title: Dietary Patterns Are Not Consistently Associated with Variability in Blood Lead Concentrations in Pregnant British Women.
ehr_evidence: The study used data from the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC) birth cohort, specifically whole blood samples and food-frequency questionnaires (FFQ).
summary: The study evaluated whether dietary patterns and specific food groups are associated with blood lead (B-Pb) concentrations in pregnant women to identify potential dietary factors influencing fetal lead exposure.
key_findings: Negative association between the 'confectionery' dietary pattern and the likelihood of B-Pb ≥5 µg/dL
Positive association between the food group 'all leafy green and green vegetables' and the likelihood of B-Pb ≥5 µg/dL
Negative association between the food group 'cakes and biscuits' and the likelihood of B-Pb ≥5 µg/dL
Positive association of the 'healthy' diet pattern with B-Pb after multiple imputation
population: Pregnant women in the Avon Longitudinal Study of Parents and Children (ALSPAC) birth cohort, UK
exposure_domain: Dietary patterns, food groups, blood lead
limitations: Lack of extensive food composition data
Variations in the lead content of foods due to growing and processing conditions
data_availability_statement: Associated DataSupplementary Materialsnxz023_Supplemental_FileClick here for additional data file.
